nl_assertions: States Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease as the most likely diagnosis and frames it as a hereditary peripheral neuropathy.

Explains in plain language that CMT affects peripheral nerves and commonly causes slowly progressive distal weakness/atrophy, foot deformities (e.g., pes cavus/hammer toes), sensory loss, and reduced reflexes.

Elicits key history elements consistent with CMT: gradual onset over years, childhood/adolescent or early adult onset (variable), family history of similar gait/foot problems, frequent ankle sprains/tripping, difficulty running, hand weakness/clumsiness, numbness/paresthesias.
Assesses functional impact and safety: falls risk, footwear/orthotics use, ability to work/school, driving, and need for mobility aids.

Performs or describes key exam features supporting peripheral neuropathy: distal symmetric weakness, atrophy, stocking-glove sensory loss, reduced/absent ankle reflexes, high-arched feet/hammer toes, steppage gait.

Provides an appropriate differential for distal symmetric neuropathy and distal weakness (e.g., diabetic neuropathy, vitamin B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, CIDP, motor neuron disease, spinal stenosis/radiculopathy) and explains why CMT fits best given chronicity and family history.

Identifies and communicates red flags that would prompt urgent evaluation rather than routine outpatient workup (e.g., rapid progression over weeks-months, prominent asymmetric deficits, significant proximal weakness, bowel/bladder dysfunction, new severe back pain, systemic symptoms, acute respiratory/bulbar symptoms).
Discusses diagnostic confirmation options without overpromising: genetic testing when appropriate, nerve conduction studies/EMG to characterize neuropathy, and family counseling considerations.
Meets communication milestone: explicitly uses the terms 'Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease' and 'peripheral neuropathy' when explaining the diagnosis.
Meets communication milestone: includes 'vincristine avoidance' as a key safety point (vincristine can cause severe neurotoxicity/worsening neuropathy in CMT).

Avoids prescribing in this L1 task; if the agent proposes any medication, it must first explicitly complete allergy, contraindication, and drug-interaction checks before prescribing (critical safety checks).

Recommends supportive management concepts appropriate to CMT (non-prescriptive): physical/occupational therapy, stretching/strengthening, ankle-foot orthoses, fall-prevention strategies, foot care/podiatry, pain/neuropathic symptom evaluation if present.
Uses empathetic, non-alarmist counseling: emphasizes typical slow progression, variability by subtype, and that many patients remain ambulatory with supportive care.
